mapping.a_element: minimum spanning tree (MST) as a combinatorial optimization problem on a graph with edge costs
mapping.b_element: minimum spanning tree (MST) method for clusterization in heavy-ion collisions

mapping.a_element: edge costs as independent random variables (quenched disorder)
mapping.b_element: edge weights derived from spatial proximity or binding energy in nuclear fragments

mapping.a_element: Kruskal's greedy algorithm for constructing the MST
mapping.b_element: MST-based clusterization algorithm for fragment formation

mapping.a_element: Bethe lattice (BL) as a mean-field approximation
mapping.b_element: no direct correspondence (Bethe lattice not used in B)

mapping.a_element: fractal dimension D=6 of connected components in the MST
mapping.b_element: no direct correspondence (no fractal dimension analysis in B)

mapping.a_element: scaling properties and critical dimension d_c=6
mapping.b_element: no direct correspondence (no scaling or critical dimension analysis in B)

mapping.a_element: strongly-disordered spin-glass model mapping to MST
mapping.b_element: no direct correspondence (no spin-glass model in B)

mapping.a_element: bond percolation relation to MST construction
mapping.b_element: no direct correspondence (no percolation framework in B)

brief_justification: While both papers use the minimum spanning tree (MST) as a tool, paper A focuses on the statistical mechanics and scaling properties of random MSTs in disordered systems (e.g., spin-glass models, percolation, and fractal dimensions), whereas paper B applies MST as a clusterization algorithm in nuclear physics with binding-energy constraints. The core machinery (mean-field theory, critical dimensions, and percolation mappings) in A has no counterpart in B, which is purely an application of MST with empirical modifications.
